summary: Reis argues that personal enthusiasm for AI tools and genuine productivity gains don't validate the massive valuations and market frenzy surrounding the industry. He positions himself as both a heavy user of AI tools and a skeptic of the bubble, drawing parallels to the dot-com era where foundational technology survived but inflated valuations didn't.
key_points: Personal ROI from AI tools doesn't justify macro-level valuations and investment
Early adopters benefiting from VC-fueled boom aren't representative of broader market
The AI bubble is primarily in startups and frontier models, less so in chips/infrastructure (for now)
AGI narrative sells better than enterprise automation reality, but LLMs likely won't achieve AGI
Expects dot-com-style outcome: foundational tech becomes utility, but most startups and valuations won't survive
money_quotes: It's possible to be both a true believer in AI's power and a deep skeptic of the industry's hype.
We are early adopters benefiting from a VC-fueled boom, and our experience is not representative of the broader market.
The pursuit of AGI makes for a better story than the reality of enterprise automation, and right now, stories are what the market is buying.
Given the Prisoner's Dilemma of Bubbles, the only game to play is to keep pumping money into the bubble.
The foundational technology will remain and become a utility, woven into everything we do. But the mountain of startups and sky-high valuations built on hype may not be so lucky.
key_insight: Data-driven practitioners should recognize that extrapolating their own positive AI experiences to justify market-wide valuations is a logical fallacy—the technology's utility and the bubble's sustainability are separate questions.
